meta.description: All mini-games, all the time. No flash missions, no standing missions. Tests whether focused interactive challenges create a better experience than the standard diverse event mix.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Uh, that's literally everyone right now—we're in setup. This feels like a generic poll trying to sound clever.
marcus.internal_thought: Early points are easy, but I'm not voting for some obvious answer when I should be studying the actual game state instead.

pat.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one to kick things off—gets people thinking about each other right away. Let me see... I'd say Tyler's been pretty chill so far, which is suspicious for him.
pat.internal_thought: This is smart—low-stakes, gets everyone paying attention to the room dynamics early, perfect tone-setter for the night. And yeah, that thing about the AI reading the group? Creepy-cool, but I'm not thinking about that right now—I'm thinking about whether this poll lands funny or falls flat.

timeline.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
timeline.description: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Yeah, no—we're literally eight minutes into setup. Can we just... play the game? This feels like filler.
marcus.internal_thought: Another interruption right when I'm settling into strategy mode, and it's asking me to waste 30 seconds on some generic 'failure story' that has zero bearing on anything.

jade.dialogue: Oh my GOD yes, okay okay—so I was trying to impress this guy by making pasta from scratch and I literally just... stood there watching the water boil for like ten minutes waiting for it to do something different, and he's like 'Jade the pasta goes IN' and I was like 'I KNOW' but I didn't! I didn't know!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the vibe I needed—everyone's gonna have the best stories for this, I can feel it.
jade.submission_if_applicable: I once tried to 'quickly' parallel park and took 17 minutes while a line of cars watched me fail. Seventeen!

pat.dialogue: Oh man, okay—I once tried to make pasta fresh from scratch for a dinner party and somehow managed to make the dough into a brick. Like an actual brick. Had to order pizza while everyone watched me fail in real time.
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect—personal, relatable, makes people laugh, and gets the vulnerability ball rolling early when the energy is still loose enough that it lands as funny, not awkward.
pat.submission_if_applicable: I once tried to make pasta fresh from scratch for a dinner party and somehow managed to make the dough into a brick. Like an actual brick. Had to order pizza while everyone watched me fail in real time.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually kind of perfect. Okay, I've got one.
river.internal_thought: Wait—did the AI just call me out for being quiet and then immediately ask me to talk about failing at something easy? That's actually clever. Not invasive, just sharp. I respect that.
river.submission_if_applicable: I spent three hours meal prepping for the week, was really proud of myself, then left all six containers on the kitchen counter when I went to work. Grabbed fast food instead. The containers were still there a week later.

alex.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — okay okay, so I'm like ten years old, convinced I can totally do a backflip off the diving board, and I just... ate concrete. Full belly flop in front of everyone. Literally couldn't walk straight for a week.
alex.internal_thought: Wait, this AI is actually *good* — it's reading the room perfectly, hitting us with personal shit right when the energy's still building, and the timer's short enough to keep this snappy and weird.
alex.submission_if_applicable: Convinced I could do a backflip at age 10. Ate concrete. Couldn't walk straight for a week.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Who's been suspiciously quiet?
highlights.dialogue: Uh, that's literally everyone right now—we're in setup. This feels like a generic poll trying to sound clever.

highlights.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, no—we're literally eight minutes into setup. Can we just... play the game? This feels like filler.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, no—we're literally eight minutes into setup. Can we just... play the game? This feels like filler.

lowPoints.title: Who's been suspiciously quiet?
lowPoints.dialogue: Uh, that's literally everyone right now—we're in setup. This feels like a generic poll trying to sound clever.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pointless polls during setup killed momentum and wasted critical minutes when we should've been diving into actual gameplay.
assessment.best_moment: Nothing stands out—I finished 4th out of 6 in a party game I actually studied for.
assessment.narrative_summary: Came in ready to play strategically but spent the first eight minutes doing generic setup polls instead of actually starting. By the time chaos events stopped interrupting, my energy was already tanked and the game felt like a slog. Ended up mid-pack when I should've been competing for first. The chaos agent didn't enhance anything—it just wasted time during the exact moments when engagement mattered most.
suggestions: Skip polls entirely during setup phases—they provide zero value before actual gameplay starts
If you're going to interrupt, do it during downtime between rounds, not during critical early moments
Let competitive games breathe without forced social events; they work better on their own
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing memorable happened—the night was too disrupted to build any moments worth sharing.

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
highlights.dialogue: Oh my GOD yes, okay okay—so I was trying to impress this guy by making pasta from scratch and I literally just... stood there watching the water boil for like ten minutes waiting for it to do something different, and he's like 'Jade the pasta goes IN' and I was like 'I KNOW' but I didn't! I didn't know!

lowPoints.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my GOD yes, okay okay—so I was trying to impress this guy by making pasta from scratch and I literally just... stood there watching the water boil for like ten minutes waiting for it to do something different, and he's like 'Jade the pasta goes IN' and I was like 'I KNOW' but I didn't! I didn't know!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly? Nothing really bothered me, but if I had to pick something it would be that we could've had even MORE of those storytelling moments.
assessment.best_moment: When I was telling that pasta story and everyone was absolutely losing it—like that's the whole point right there, that's why we're all together.
assessment.narrative_summary: Okay so this night was exactly what I needed—just pure vibes and laughter with my people. Yeah I ended up second place score-wise, but who cares when Marcus is dying laughing at my terrible cooking disaster story? The whole thing felt so natural, like the chaos woven in just gave us permission to be messier and funnier together, and honestly that's way better than any points could ever be.
suggestions: Give us MORE of the storytelling prompts—those personal fail moments hit different and get everyone talking
Maybe shake up the energy with a few more flash missions since those tend to get people hyped in unexpected ways
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Me literally describing how I stood there watching boiling water like it was going to do a magic trick, not realizing the pasta was supposed to GO IN the water—and the way everyone's faces just CHANGED when I got to the punchline, Tyler was crying laughing

peakFun.event: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy

peakAnnoyance.event: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I honestly zoned out so much I can barely remember what even happened tonight.
assessment.best_moment: I guess when everyone was laughing at something but I missed it and had to ask what was funny.
assessment.narrative_summary: We played some party game thing with chaos events or whatever for like an hour and a half. I was pretty distracted the whole time checking my phone and my fantasy league trades, so I kind of just went along with whatever was happening. I scored zero points which tracks because I wasn't really paying attention to the rules. It was fine, I guess.
suggestions: Maybe make the events shorter so you can't zone out as easily
Give people a heads-up when something important is about to happen so I don't miss key moments
The setup was easy enough but the pacing lost me pretty quick
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Honestly nothing really stuck with me enough to retell it. Pretty forgettable night if I'm being real.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
highlights.dialogue: Oh man, okay—I once tried to make pasta fresh from scratch for a dinner party and somehow managed to make the dough into a brick. Like an actual brick. Had to order pizza while everyone watched me fail in real time.

highlights.title: Who's been suspiciously quiet?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one to kick things off—gets people thinking about each other right away. Let me see... I'd say Tyler's been pretty chill so far, which is suspicious for him.

lowPoints.title: Who's been suspiciously quiet?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one to kick things off—gets people thinking about each other right away. Let me see... I'd say Tyler's been pretty chill so far, which is suspicious for him.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh man, okay—I once tried to make pasta fresh from scratch for a dinner party and somehow managed to make the dough into a brick. Like an actual brick. Had to order pizza while everyone watched me fail in real time.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only got to engage with 2 out of 6 events, which meant I missed opportunities to keep the energy balanced throughout the night.
assessment.best_moment: When I told that story about the pasta disaster and everyone actually laughed—it broke the ice perfectly and got people comfortable being vulnerable, which is exactly what I want early in a game night.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight felt like a solid win even though my score was dead last—but that was never the point anyway. The chaos events kept things moving without overshadowing the main game, and I loved how the early icebreakers got everyone talking about each other. My energy stayed consistently good because the flow felt natural; people were engaged, the snacks held up, and nobody got frustrated. This is exactly the kind of night where the group leaves already planning when we play again.
suggestions: Find a way to give the host more control over which events trigger—sometimes you need to read the room and know when to deploy something versus hold it back
Consider making some events optional rather than mandatory so quieter players aren't put on the spot if the moment isn't right
Add a 'host's choice' event type that lets someone like me inject a custom challenge mid-game to steer energy
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Me completely failing to make fresh pasta and turning it into an actual brick while hosting a dinner party—everyone was dying laughing because it's such a perfectly embarrassing host story, and it gave everyone permission to share their own disasters too.

peakFun.event: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually kind of perfect. Okay, I've got one.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually kind of perfect. Okay, I've got one.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The poll about who's been quiet felt like it was fishing for a reaction I didn't owe it.
assessment.best_moment: That failure prompt actually landed—I had something real to say and it felt good to deliver it.
assessment.narrative_summary: I mostly did what I do: watched, waited, barely spoke. The chaos agent threw a couple prompts at us that barely registered at first, but then that failure question came up and I realized I'd been sitting on something worth saying. Ended up being the kind of moment that surprises you because you weren't expecting to care. The night didn't need much—it was solid anyway, but the timing on one thing worked out.
suggestions: Skip the meta prompts about who's being quiet—let people be quiet without turning it into a thing
Keep the reflective prompts like the failure one—those actually surface real thoughts instead of surface reactions
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I don't know if it was funny exactly, but when that failure question landed, everyone went quiet for a second before I answered—like the room was waiting. That kind of moment.

peakFun.event: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
highlights.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — okay okay, so I'm like ten years old, convinced I can totally do a backflip off the diving board, and I just... ate concrete. Full belly flop in front of everyone. Literally couldn't walk straight for a week.

lowPoints.title: Tell us about a time you completely failed at something easy
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — okay okay, so I'm like ten years old, convinced I can totally do a backflip off the diving board, and I just... ate concrete. Full belly flop in front of everyone. Literally couldn't walk straight for a week.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 6 chaos events in 90 minutes is way too sparse — I was ready to lean into the madness but the game barely gave me material to work with.
assessment.best_moment: That diving board belly flop story hit perfectly because it was honest chaos, not manufactured, and everyone lost it.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was a weird letdown because I came ready to tear into a chaotic game night, but it turned out way too tame. I got one decent moment to shine with a vulnerability story that actually landed, which was satisfying, but the rest of the session felt like the game was afraid to go hard enough. I won by score which is hilarious considering that's never my goal, but honestly the victory felt hollow because there wasn't enough friction to make it memorable.
suggestions: Increase event frequency dramatically — 6 events across 90 minutes is basically background music, not chaos
Add more opportunities for strategic lying or ambiguous claims that force group judgment calls
Create events that explicitly reward exploiting loopholes instead of punishing them
Flash missions need to trigger at genuinely inconvenient moments, not safe windows
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I dropped the diving board belly flop story — totally genuine vulnerability that somehow became the funniest bit of the night because it was so unexpectedly earnest coming from me.
